description: Build the blockchain education presentation slide by slide as a polished Vite + React deck, with one implementable story for every parent slide and subslide in the current presentation map.

userStories.title: Build the chapter intro for What is a blockchain
userStories.description: As a learner, I want the opening slide for blockchain basics to frame the chapter with a clear headline, supporting pillars, and a strong takeaway before the subslides begin.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 01 renders as a parent chapter slide for What is a blockchain
The slide communicates shared record, rules before trust, and hard to rewrite as the three core ideas
The layout feels presentation-ready and matches the deck-wide visual system
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 01.1 Shared ledger basics
userStories.description: As a learner, I want a comparison between a traditional database and a blockchain ledger so I understand why a shared record matters.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 01.1 explains the shared ledger concept in plain language
The slide contrasts centralized record-keeping with a replicated blockchain ledger
A simple step flow shows how an update becomes part of common history
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 01.2 Blockchain in motion
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to see the chain advancing visually so I can internalize how new validated blocks extend the record over time.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 01.2 presents the blockchain as an animated sequence of blocks moving forward
The motion clearly shows older blocks shifting back while the newest block arrives
Supporting labels explain transactions gathering, validation, and chain advancement
Verify in browser
userStories.notes: Use a Remotion-driven animation similar to the current blockchain in motion slide.

userStories.title: Build slide 01.3 How nodes work
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to understand how nodes verify and relay information so the network can converge without one central server.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 01.3 explains what a node is and what it does
The slide contrasts centralized validation with peer-to-peer verification
The flow from wallet to peer relay to network convergence is clear
Verify in browser
userStories.notes: Add a Remotion animation if the network relay concept needs motion to read clearly, using the same approach as the blockchain motion slide.

userStories.title: Build slide 01.4 Blocks, validators, and finality
userStories.description: As a learner, I want a pipeline view from transactions to finality so I understand how activity becomes reliable history.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 01.4 shows the stages from transactions to blocks to validators to finality
Each stage has concise educational copy
The slide defines finality in a way that is understandable to beginners
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build the chapter intro for Bitcoin
userStories.description: As a learner, I want the Bitcoin chapter to establish why Bitcoin exists and what topics the next set of slides will cover.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 02 renders as a parent chapter slide for Bitcoin
The slide introduces Bitcoin as the first major blockchain application and frames the chapter arc
The layout leaves the deck feeling cohesive with chapter 01
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 02.1 The problem Bitcoin solves
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to understand the double-spend and trust problem so Bitcoin feels like an answer to a real issue.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 02.1 explains the problem of digital scarcity and trusted intermediaries
The slide makes clear why double-spending is hard to solve online
The design stays simple enough for first-time learners
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 02.2 Satoshi and the white paper
userStories.description: As a learner, I want context on Satoshi Nakamoto and the white paper so I can place Bitcoin historically without losing focus on the ideas.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 02.2 introduces Satoshi Nakamoto and the white paper at a high level
The slide anchors Bitcoin's launch in its historical moment
The copy stays concise and presentation-friendly rather than biographical
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 02.3 What is a block?
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to know what information a Bitcoin block contains so the rest of the chapter has a concrete mental model.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 02.3 explains a block as a batch of ordered transactions plus metadata
The slide visually distinguishes transactions, header data, and block identity
The explanation supports the later hashing and mining slides
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 02.4 The chain and hashing
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to see how blocks link through hashes so I understand why changing past data breaks the chain.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 02.4 explains hashing and block linkage in beginner-friendly language
The slide shows how one block references the previous block
The design makes tampering consequences easy to grasp visually
Verify in browser
userStories.notes: Add a Remotion animation if needed to show hash links updating or breaking across a chain.

userStories.title: Build slide 02.5 Mining and halving
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to understand how miners compete to add blocks and why halvings change Bitcoin issuance over time.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 02.5 explains mining as block production under Proof of Work
The slide explains what a halving is and why it matters to issuance
The content avoids jargon overload while preserving technical accuracy
Verify in browser
userStories.notes: Add a Remotion animation if useful for showing issuance stepping down over successive halvings.

userStories.title: Build slide 02.6 21M hard cap
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to understand Bitcoin's fixed supply so I can connect its monetary policy to its design.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 02.6 explains the idea of a fixed 21 million supply
The slide connects the hard cap to issuance and halvings
The visual treatment keeps the idea memorable without excess decoration
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 02.7 Nodes and decentralization
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to know why node operators matter so decentralization feels operational rather than abstract.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 02.7 explains the role of nodes in enforcing Bitcoin's rules
The slide connects node distribution to censorship resistance and resilience
The copy distinguishes nodes from miners clearly
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 02.8 Wallets and keys
userStories.description: As a learner, I want a simple mental model for wallets and keys so I know what users actually control.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 02.8 explains public and private key roles at a beginner level
The slide clarifies that wallets manage keys and signing, not coins stored in an app
The layout is clean enough to support later wallet and EOA slides
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 02.9 Transactions and mempool
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to understand where transactions wait before confirmation so pending activity makes sense.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 02.9 explains the mempool as the waiting area for valid unconfirmed transactions
The slide connects sending a transaction to miner selection and confirmation
The explanation stays distinct from Ethereum gas mechanics covered later
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build the chapter intro for Ethereum
userStories.description: As a learner, I want the Ethereum chapter opener to establish the shift from digital money to a programmable blockchain.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 03 renders as a parent chapter slide for Ethereum
The slide frames Ethereum as extending blockchain utility beyond simple transfers
The chapter intro prepares the audience for state, the EVM, and smart contracts
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 03.1 What Ethereum adds beyond Bitcoin
userStories.description: As a learner, I want a clear comparison between Bitcoin and Ethereum so I understand why Ethereum exists.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 03.1 explains Ethereum's broader programmability compared with Bitcoin
The slide keeps the comparison accurate without overstating either system
The visual structure makes the contrast easy to present live
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 03.2 State, accounts, and the EVM
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to understand Ethereum's state machine and execution environment so smart contracts are easier to grasp.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 03.2 explains accounts, shared state, and the EVM in accessible terms
The slide shows that transactions update shared state through execution
The content creates a strong bridge to the smart contracts chapter
Verify in browser
userStories.notes: Add a Remotion animation if needed to show state transitions flowing through the EVM.

userStories.title: Build slide 03.3 Smart contracts and programmability
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to see what programmable logic on Ethereum enables so contracts feel useful rather than abstract.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 03.3 explains smart contracts as code that executes on-chain
The slide includes concrete examples of programmable behaviors
The slide sets up the next chapter cleanly
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build the chapter intro for Smart Contracts
userStories.description: As a learner, I want the smart contracts chapter opener to frame contracts as deterministic software with real constraints and risks.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 04 renders as a parent chapter slide for Smart Contracts
The slide introduces contracts as on-chain programs with rules and outcomes
The presentation tone stays educational rather than promotional
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 04.1 How contracts execute
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to understand the lifecycle of a contract call so execution stops feeling magical.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 04.1 explains deployment, calling a function, execution, and state updates
The slide makes clear that every node executes the same logic
The narrative remains beginner-friendly without losing the deterministic model
Verify in browser
userStories.notes: Add a Remotion animation if needed to show a contract call moving from user input to state change.

userStories.title: Build slide 04.2 Common examples and risks
userStories.description: As a learner, I want examples of what contracts can do and what can go wrong so the tradeoffs are concrete.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 04.2 includes practical contract examples such as tokens, escrow, or governance
The slide introduces key risks like bugs, exploits, and upgrade complexity
The content balances capability with caution
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build the chapter intro for Ethereum Values and CROPS
userStories.description: As a learner, I want this chapter to introduce Ethereum's cultural values before diving into the CROPS framework.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 05 renders as a parent chapter slide for Ethereum values
The slide frames values as design principles that shape the ecosystem
The chapter opener fits the same visual language as the rest of the deck
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 05.1 What CROPS stands for
userStories.description: As a learner, I want a clear explanation of the CROPS acronym so I can remember the value framework.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 05.1 defines each letter in CROPS clearly
The visual layout helps the audience scan and remember the framework
The wording stays specific to Ethereum ecosystem values
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 05.2 How values show up in ecosystem design
userStories.description: As a learner, I want examples of how Ethereum values influence products and governance so the framework feels real.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 05.2 ties CROPS principles to visible ecosystem design choices
The slide shows at least a few concrete manifestations of those values
The content avoids drifting into vague slogans
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build the chapter intro for DeFi use-cases
userStories.description: As a learner, I want the DeFi chapter opener to frame decentralized finance as a set of on-chain financial building blocks.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 06 renders as a parent chapter slide for DeFi use-cases
The slide introduces DeFi without assuming prior finance expertise
The chapter opener prepares the audience for both opportunities and risks
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 06.1 Swaps, lending, and stablecoins
userStories.description: As a learner, I want a clear tour of core DeFi primitives so I can recognize the main categories of on-chain finance.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 06.1 explains swaps, lending, and stablecoins at a conceptual level
The slide distinguishes the role each primitive plays in DeFi
The examples are concrete enough to support later discussion of composability
Verify in browser
userStories.notes: Add a Remotion animation if useful for showing assets moving across protocols in one user journey.

userStories.title: Build slide 06.2 Risks, composability, and real-world fit
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to understand why DeFi is powerful but fragile so I can evaluate use-cases more critically.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 06.2 explains composability as protocols building on each other
The slide highlights meaningful risks such as liquidations, smart contract bugs, and market structure issues
The content discusses where DeFi may or may not fit real-world needs
Verify in browser
userStories.notes: Add a Remotion animation if needed to illustrate composability chains or cascading risk.

userStories.title: Build the chapter intro for the Ethereum community
userStories.description: As a learner, I want the community chapter opener to frame Ethereum as a social and technical ecosystem rather than a single company.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 07 renders as a parent chapter slide for the Ethereum community
The slide introduces the idea of a many-sided ecosystem with different roles
The opener keeps the tone grounded and explanatory
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 07.1 Core teams, researchers, and builders
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to see the main actor groups in Ethereum so the ecosystem has a human shape.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 07.1 identifies core teams, researchers, client teams, and builders
The slide shows how these groups contribute differently
The layout helps the audience understand roles without oversimplifying them
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 07.2 How the ecosystem coordinates change
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to understand how Ethereum evolves without a single central controller.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 07.2 explains rough coordination across discussions, research, client implementation, and upgrades
The slide shows that change emerges through social and technical processes together
The content stays high-level enough for a presentation audience
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build the chapter intro for Gas and transaction fees
userStories.description: As a learner, I want the gas chapter opener to frame fees as part of network resource management, not just a nuisance.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 08 renders as a parent chapter slide for gas and fees
The slide introduces the idea that computation and block space are scarce resources
The opener sets up both why fees exist and how users experience them
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 08.1 Why gas exists
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to understand gas as a pricing mechanism for computation so fees feel principled rather than arbitrary.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 08.1 explains gas as a way to meter computation and storage usage
The slide connects gas to spam resistance and fair resource allocation
The explanation stays distinct from total fee calculation details
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 08.2 Fee market and user experience
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to see how fee markets affect what users pay and how transactions feel in practice.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 08.2 explains that users compete for block space through fee bidding mechanics
The slide connects congestion to user-facing fee volatility and waiting time
The design keeps the concept teachable without too much protocol detail
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build the chapter intro for Wallets and Keys / EOA
userStories.description: As a learner, I want the wallet chapter opener to frame identity and control on Ethereum before details about EOAs and custody.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 09 renders as a parent chapter slide for wallets and EOAs
The slide introduces wallet control, signing, and custody as the core themes
The opener maintains consistency with the rest of the deck
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 09.1 EOAs, seed phrases, and signing
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to understand how EOAs and seed phrases relate to signatures so wallet security has a technical basis.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 09.1 explains EOAs, seed phrases, and signing in clear beginner language
The slide distinguishes account abstraction ideas from classic EOAs only if necessary for clarity
The content reinforces safe mental models around key material
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 09.2 Custody models and wallet UX
userStories.description: As a learner, I want a comparison of self-custody and custodial models so the tradeoffs are explicit.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 09.2 compares self-custody, custodial, and hybrid wallet patterns
The slide connects security tradeoffs to user experience choices
The presentation remains practical and non-dogmatic
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build the chapter intro for Consensus
userStories.description: As a learner, I want the consensus chapter opener to frame agreement as the core problem of decentralized systems.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 10 renders as a parent chapter slide for consensus
The slide introduces consensus as the process of agreeing on valid history
The opener prepares the audience for Proof of Work and Proof of Stake tradeoffs
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 10.1 Proof of Work vs Proof of Stake
userStories.description: As a learner, I want a side-by-side explanation of Proof of Work and Proof of Stake so the tradeoffs are easier to compare.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 10.1 explains the core mechanism of Proof of Work and Proof of Stake
The slide presents the comparison in a balanced, non-caricatured way
The visual treatment helps the audience compare security resources and participation models
Verify in browser
userStories.notes: Add a Remotion animation if needed to make the two consensus flows legible in a single comparison.

userStories.title: Build slide 10.2 Security assumptions and tradeoffs
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to understand what each consensus model assumes about attackers and incentives so the comparison has depth.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 10.2 explains the major security assumptions behind consensus choices
The slide includes tradeoffs such as capital, energy, coordination, or attack surfaces as appropriate
The content stays understandable without flattening the nuance completely
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build the chapter intro for Security and OPSEC
userStories.description: As a learner, I want the final chapter opener to shift the deck from concepts to practical self-protection.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 11 renders as a parent chapter slide for security and OPSEC
The slide introduces security as both technical and behavioral
The opener sets up a practical closing section for the presentation
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 11.1 Common attack surfaces
userStories.description: As a learner, I want to see the main ways users get exploited so I can recognize risk patterns early.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 11.1 covers common attack surfaces such as phishing, malicious approvals, and compromised devices or sites
The slide keeps examples practical and current enough to be useful
The content remains high-signal for a live presentation
Verify in browser

userStories.title: Build slide 11.2 Practical safety habits
userStories.description: As a learner, I want a concise checklist of habits that reduce risk so the presentation ends with actionable guidance.
acceptanceCriteria: Slide 11.2 presents a practical set of OPSEC habits for everyday users
The slide emphasizes repeatable behaviors rather than vague warnings
The final slide lands as a strong, useful close to the deck
Verify in browser
